message:
chore: Remove unused imports and simplify tsconfig

UNUSED IMPORT CLEANUP:
- src/tracking.ts: Remove unused ProcessedEntrySchema import
- src/utils.ts: Remove unused ScoredBullet, CassSearchHit, PlaybookBullet imports
  (ContextResult is the only type needed)

TSCONFIG SIMPLIFICATION:
- Enable composite: true for project references support
- Remove explicit typeRoots and types arrays (use defaults)
- Remove explicit include/exclude (use TypeScript defaults)
- Add trailing newline for POSIX compliance

WHY:
Cleaner imports reduce cognitive load. The tsconfig simplification removes
redundant configuration that TypeScript handles well with defaults.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"cass_memory_system-1jdn","title":"[Foundation] Define ~/.cass-memory/ directory structure","description":"Define and implement the ~/.cass-memory/ global directory structure for user-level CASS Memory System data.

## Context
The ~/.cass-memory/ directory is the user's global memory store, containing personal configuration, playbooks, diary entries, and usage tracking. This directory persists across all projects and repositories the user works on, serving as their personal agent memory layer.

## Technical Specification

### Directory Structure:
~/.cass-memory/
├── config.json              # User configuration (API keys, default model, etc.)
├── playbook.yaml            # Personal global playbook (rules across all projects)
├── toxic_bullets.log        # Permanently blocked patterns (user-level)
├── diary/                   # Diary entries organized by date
│   ├── 2025-12-07.md
│   ├── 2025-12-06.md
│   └── ...
├── reflections/             # Processed session tracking
│   ├── session-abc123.json
│   ├── session-def456.json
│   └── ...
├── usage.jsonl              # Bullet usage log with timestamps
├── embeddings/              # Local vector store for semantic search
│   ├── index.bin
│   └── metadata.json
└── cost/                    # LLM cost tracking
    ├── monthly-2025-12.jsonl
    └── total.json

### File Specifications:

#### config.json:
- llm_provider: 'openai' | 'anthropic' | 'google'
- model: string (e.g., 'gpt-4', 'claude-opus-4-5', 'gemini-pro')
- api_key: string (primary API key)
- api_keys: { openai?: string, anthropic?: string, google?: string }
- max_tokens: number (default response length)
- temperature: number (0-1, creativity setting)
- enable_curation: boolean (auto-curate bullets)
- evidence_threshold: number (usage count before LLM curation)
- enable_sanitization: boolean (auto-detect secrets)

#### playbook.yaml:
- bullets: Array of global rules (applies to ALL projects)
- categories: SETUP, BUILD, TEST, DEBUG, DEPLOY, etc.
- each bullet has: content, context, priority, state, usage_count

#### toxic_bullets.log:
- Newline-delimited list of blocked bullet content
- Case-insensitive matching
- Applied globally to prevent toxic rules

#### diary/ (daily markdown files):
- One file per day: YYYY-MM-DD.md
- Timestamped entries
- Free-form text + metadata frontmatter
- Used for reflection and pattern extraction

#### reflections/ (session metadata):
- One JSON file per session
- session_id, timestamp, repo_path, command, duration
- extracted_patterns: Array of learned behaviors
- applied_bullets: Array of bullets used in session

#### usage.jsonl (bullet tracking):
- One line per bullet usage
- timestamp, bullet_hash, repo_path, session_id, outcome
- Used for evidence-based curation
- Queryable for historical analysis

#### embeddings/ (vector store):
- Binary index for fast semantic search
- Metadata mapping IDs to bullet content
- Future: enable natural language playbook queries

#### cost/ (LLM usage tracking):
- Monthly JSONL files: timestamp, provider, model, tokens, cost
- total.json: cumulative spend across all providers
- Helps users budget LLM usage

## Reasoning

### Why Global Directory:
1. Cross-Project Memory: Rules learned in one project apply to others
2. User Ownership: User controls their personal memory store
3. Privacy: Personal diary and usage data stays local
4. Portability: Backup and restore entire memory with one directory

### Why config.json:
1. Single Source of Truth: All LLM configuration in one place
2. API Key Security: Stored locally, never committed to git
3. User Preferences: Default model and behavior settings
4. Easy Onboarding: cass-memory init walks through setup

### Why playbook.yaml:
1. Human-Readable: YAML is easy to edit manually
2. Version-Controllable: Can commit to dotfiles repo
3. Mergeable: Combines with repo-level playbooks
4. Hierarchical: Categories organize rules logically

### Why toxic_bullets.log:
1. Safety Net: Prevents re-learning bad patterns
2. Permanent: Once blocked, stays blocked
3. User Control: Users curate their own blocklist
4. Simple Format: Plain text, easy to inspect/edit

### Why diary/:
1. Human-Readable: Markdown for manual review
2. Daily Organization: Natural time-based structure
3. Reflection Source: LLM mines diary for patterns
4. Debugging: Understand what agent was thinking

### Why reflections/:
1. Session Tracking: Know what agent learned when
2. Reproducibility: Trace which bullets were applied
3. Analytics: Measure bullet effectiveness
4. Audit Trail: Compliance and debugging

### Why usage.jsonl:
1. Evidence-Based: Track which rules are actually useful
2. Queryable: Filter by repo, time range, outcome
3. Curation Input: LLM uses usage data for decisions
4. Append-Only: Fast writes, no corruption risk

### Why embeddings/:
1. Semantic Search: Find similar rules without exact matches
2. Local-First: No cloud dependency for search
3. Future-Proof: Enables natural language queries
4. Privacy: Embeddings stay on user's machine

### Why cost/:
1. Budget Tracking: Know how much LLM usage costs
2. Provider Comparison: See which provider is most cost-effective
3. Monthly Reports: Track spending trends
4. Transparency: Users see exactly what they're paying

## Acceptance Criteria

- ~/.cass-memory/ directory is created on cass-memory init
- All subdirectories created (diary, reflections, embeddings, cost)
- config.json has sensible defaults if not present
- playbook.yaml created with empty bullets array
- toxic_bullets.log created (empty file)
- usage.jsonl created (empty file)
- Permissions set correctly (user read/write only for config.json)
- Directory creation is idempotent (safe to run multiple times)
- Helper functions in utils.ts: resolveGlobalDir(), ensureGlobalStructure()
- cass-memory doctor verifies directory structure

## How This Serves the Universal Agent Memory System

The ~/.cass-memory/ directory is the user's personal memory layer:

1. Persistence: Memory survives across projects, jobs, and machines
2. Privacy: Personal diary and usage data never leaves user's machine
3. Portability: Backup entire memory with one directory
4. Cross-Project Learning: Rules learned in project A apply to project B
5. User Control: Users own and curate their memory
6. Multi-Agent Support: Future agents can read from same memory store

This global directory is the foundation for the universal agent memory vision. Without it, agents would be memoryless, relearning the same patterns in every project.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:47:22.033613-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:47:22.033613-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-1sp","title":"[Curator] handleMergeDelta - Combine similar bullets","description":"Combine multiple similar bullets into a single consolidated rule.

Signature:
function handleMergeDelta(
  playbook: Playbook,
  delta: MergeDelta
): { applied: boolean; newBulletId?: string }

Process:
1. Find all bullets by delta.bulletIds
   - If any not found: return applied: false
2. Aggregate feedback from all bullets:
   - Concatenate helpfulEvents arrays
   - Concatenate harmfulEvents arrays
   - Sum helpfulCount
   - Sum harmfulCount
3. Create new merged bullet:
   - content: delta.mergedContent
   - category: inherit from highest-maturity source
   - kind: inherit from highest-maturity source
   - maturity: max(source maturities)
   - state: 'active'
   - helpfulCount: aggregated total
   - harmfulCount: aggregated total
   - helpfulEvents: aggregated array
   - harmfulEvents: aggregated array
   - createdAt: earliest of source bullets
   - updatedAt: current timestamp
4. Deprecate all source bullets:
   - For each source: handleDeprecateDelta
   - Set deprecationReason: 'merged'
   - Set replacedBy: new bullet ID
5. Return applied: true, newBulletId

Why merge?
- Consolidate duplicate rules with slightly different wording
- Combine partial rules into comprehensive guidance
- Reduce playbook bloat

Feedback aggregation preserves trust earned across all sources.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:02.189871-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T17:14:47.626201-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-1v63","title":"[Tracking] Usage analytics tracking","description":"Purpose: Track usage patterns for internal analytics and debugging

File path: ~/.cass-memory/usage.jsonl

Format: JSON Lines (one JSON object per line)

Example entries:
```jsonl
{"timestamp":"2025-12-07T10:30:00Z","event":"bullet_marked","data":{"bulletId":"rule-123","feedback":"helpful"}}
{"timestamp":"2025-12-07T10:35:00Z","event":"command_run","data":{"command":"reflect","scope":"workspace","duration_ms":2340}}
{"timestamp":"2025-12-07T11:00:00Z","event":"session_count","data":{"provider":"claude","count":15}}
{"timestamp":"2025-12-07T11:30:00Z","event":"bullet_marked","data":{"bulletId":"rule-456","feedback":"harmful"}}
```

Tracked events:
- bullet_marked: User feedback on playbook bullets (helpful/harmful)
- command_run: Which commands executed and performance
- session_count: Number of sessions discovered per provider
- reflection_stats: Deltas proposed vs applied

Analytics use cases:
- Which bullets are most helpful/harmful
- Command usage patterns
- System performance over time
- Reflection effectiveness

Privacy: All local, never transmitted","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:51:20.699623-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:51:20.699623-05:00"}
-{"id":"cass_memory_system-1vb","title":"[Foundation] .gitignore for Bun/TypeScript project","description":"Create .gitignore file with comprehensive ignore patterns for Bun, TypeScript, and CASS Memory System artifacts.

## Context
The .gitignore file prevents committing build artifacts, dependencies, secrets, and temporary files to version control. This is critical for security, repository cleanliness, and avoiding merge conflicts.

## Technical Specification

### Ignore Patterns:

#### Dependencies:
node_modules/
.pnp.*
.yarn/

#### Build Output:
dist/
build/
*.js (in src/ - TypeScript source only)
*.d.ts
*.tsbuildinfo

#### Environment & Secrets:
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local
config.json (if it contains API keys)
*.key
*.pem
*.p12

#### Logs:
*.log
npm-debug.log*
yarn-debug.log*
yarn-error.log*
lerna-debug.log*
pnpm-debug.log*

#### Testing:
coverage/
.nyc_output/
*.lcov

#### IDE & OS:
.DS_Store
.DS_Store?
._*
.Spotlight-V100
.Trashes
ehthumbs.db
Thumbs.db
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp
*.swo
*~

#### Bun Specific:
bun.lockb (optional - some teams commit this)

#### CASS Memory System Specific:
.cass/config.yaml (may contain local overrides with secrets)
.cass/toxic.log (project-specific, should be committed)
diary/*.md (personal diary entries - never commit)
usage.jsonl (personal usage data)

## Reasoning

### Why Ignore node_modules:
1. Size: Can be hundreds of MB
2. Reproducibility: package.json and lock file are sufficient
3. Platform-specific: Some packages compile binaries per OS

### Why Ignore dist/:
1. Build Artifacts: Generated from source, not source itself
2. Reproducibility: bun run build recreates it
3. Binary Size: Compiled binaries are large

### Why Ignore .env:
1. Security: Contains API keys and secrets
2. Local Config: Each developer has their own keys
3. Production Safety: Prevents accidental secret commits

### Why Ignore Logs:
1. Noise: Logs change frequently
2. Size: Can grow large quickly
3. Local: Each developer has their own logs

### Why Ignore IDE Files:
1. Personal Preference: Each dev uses different editors
2. Settings Drift: IDE settings are personal
3. Repository Cleanliness: Not relevant to project

### Why NOT Ignore .cass/playbook.yaml:
1. Memory as Code: Playbook is part of the project
2. Team Sharing: All devs should see project-specific rules
3. Version Control: Track changes to team knowledge

### Why NOT Ignore .cass/toxic.log:
1. Shared Blocklist: Team should share blocked patterns
2. Safety: Prevents toxic rules from spreading

## Acceptance Criteria

- .gitignore exists in project root
- All standard patterns included (node_modules, dist, .env, logs)
- OS-specific patterns included (.DS_Store, Thumbs.db)
- IDE patterns included (.vscode, .idea, *.swp)
- Bun patterns included if needed
- CASS-specific patterns included
- Comments explain each section
- git status shows only relevant files after build
- Secrets are never tracked (test with dummy .env)

## How This Serves the Universal Agent Memory System

The .gitignore file protects the integrity and security of the CASS Memory System:

1. Security: Prevents accidental API key commits that could leak to LLM providers
2. Collaboration: Keeps repository clean for team members
3. CI/CD: Ensures builds are reproducible without committed artifacts
4. Privacy: Keeps personal diary entries and usage data local
5. Memory as Code: Ensures playbooks are committed while secrets are not

Without proper .gitignore, the repository would be polluted with build artifacts, secrets would be at risk of exposure, and team collaboration would suffer from constant merge conflicts in generated files.","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:46:41.595261-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T20:09:09.469977-05:00"}

## How This Serves the Universal Agent Memory System

The .gitignore file protects the integrity and security of the CASS Memory System:

1. Security: Prevents accidental API key commits that could leak to LLM providers
2. Collaboration: Keeps repository clean for team members
3. CI/CD: Ensures builds are reproducible without committed artifacts
4. Privacy: Keeps personal diary entries and usage data local
5. Memory as Code: Ensures playbooks are committed while secrets are not

Without proper .gitignore, the repository would be polluted with build artifacts, secrets would be at risk of exposure, and team collaboration would suffer from constant merge conflicts in generated files.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:46:41.595261-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T20:15:52.186125-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-07T20:15:43.159247-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-25k","title":"[Utils] daysSince - Days elapsed since timestamp","description":"","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:12.01385-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:12.01385-05:00","comments":[{"id":1,"issue_id":"cass_memory_system-25k","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Function Signature
```typescript
function daysSince(isoDate: string): number
function daysSince(timestamp: number): number
```

## Implementation Details
- Overloaded function accepting ISO string or timestamp
- If string, parse to Date, get timestamp via getTime()
- Calculate (Date.now() - timestamp) in milliseconds
- Convert to days: ms / (1000 * 60 * 60 * 24)
- Return as number (can be fractional)

## Why This Matters
Critical for confidence decay calculation. Used to determine how old a bullet is for scoring. Fractional days allow fine-grained decay (e.g., 1.5 days old).

## Edge Cases to Handle
- Future dates - returns negative number
- Invalid ISO string - throw error
- Zero timestamp - returns large positive number
- Very old dates - returns very large number
- Same timestamp - returns 0","created_at":"2025-12-07T23:15:50Z"}]}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-27y","title":"[Playbook] pinBullet/unpinBullet - Protect bullet from auto-prune","description":"## Function Signature
```typescript
function pinBullet(playbook: Playbook, bulletId: string, reason: string): void
function unpinBullet(playbook: Playbook, bulletId: string): void
```

## Purpose
Protect critical bullets from auto-pruning. Pinned bullets are NEVER automatically deprecated, even if they accumulate harmful feedback.

## Pin Implementation
```typescript
const bullet = findBullet(playbook, bulletId);
if (!bullet) throw new Error(`Bullet not found: ${bulletId}`);

bullet.pinned = true;
bullet.pinnedReason = reason;
bullet.updatedAt = now();
```

## Unpin Implementation
```typescript
const bullet = findBullet(playbook, bulletId);
if (!bullet) throw new Error(`Bullet not found: ${bulletId}`);

bullet.pinned = false;
bullet.pinnedReason = undefined;
bullet.updatedAt = now();
```

## Why Pin Bullets?
1. **Critical organizational rules**: Never prune core principles
2. **Regulatory requirements**: Legal/compliance rules must stay
3. **Prevent accidental removal**: Protect proven valuable rules
4. **Override auto-prune**: Keep despite negative feedback

## Common Pin Reasons
- "Core organizational principle"
- "Regulatory compliance requirement"
- "Explicitly requested by team lead"
- "Historical knowledge - must preserve"
- "Critical security guideline"

## Auto-Prune Protection
```typescript
function shouldPrune(bullet: PlaybookBullet): boolean {
  if (bullet.pinned) return false;  // NEVER prune pinned
  
  // Check harmful threshold, etc...
}
```

## Pin vs High Priority
- **Pinned**: Never auto-pruned, preserved indefinitely
- **High maturity**: Can still be pruned if harmful
- **Both**: Pinned overrides all auto-prune logic

## Usage Scenarios
1. **Manual pin**: User runs `cm pin {bulletId} --reason="..."`
2. **Migration**: Pin critical rules during schema upgrades
3. **Organizational policy**: Pin company-wide standards
4. **Recovery**: Pin after accidental auto-prune

## Implementation Notes
- Check pinned status BEFORE any auto-prune logic
- Store reason for audit trail
- Allow unpinning when no longer needed
- Show pinned status in stats/list commands

## Dependencies
- findBullet()

## Related Functions
- autoPrune() - checks pinned status
- getActiveBullets() - includes pinned bullets
- deprecateBullet() - manual deprecation still works","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:45:09.305217-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T20:09:09.473109-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-07T20:08:10.011741-05:00"}
 {"id":"cass_memory_system-2hi3","title":"[MCP:Future] MCP request handler implementation","description":"Implement MCP request handler:

```typescript
server.setRequestHandler("tools/call", async (request) => {
  switch (request.params.name) {
    case "memory_context":
      return getContext(request.params.arguments);
    case "memory_mark":
      return markBullet(request.params.arguments);
    case "memory_search":
      return searchMemory(request.params.arguments);
    case "memory_reflect":
      return triggerReflect(request.params.arguments);
    default:
      throw new Error(\`Unknown tool: ${request.params.name}\`);
  }
});
```

Routes tool calls to appropriate handler functions.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-07T16:49:33.289055-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-07T17:15:02.209999-05:00"}
